Research

Assignments, Final Poster & Publication

Everything you're responsible for — due dates, formats, and what happens after

Key Milestones at a Glance

Fri Jul 3Project selection deadline — Slack Priscilla: which project (P1–P6) you're choosing and your draft research question.
Wed Jul 9Project proposal presentations — 5-min oral pitch of your selected project, method, and plan.
Fri Jul 111-page project summary due — written proposal with RQ, method, hypothesis, and week-by-week milestones.
Wed Jul 16Mid-point check-in — show real results so far, confirm scope is achievable.
Fri Jul 25Poster draft due — all sections filled, main result visible. Feedback returned by Mon Jul 28.
Mon Jul 28Poster inspection — 1:1 review before print. Revisions due Tue Jul 29 noon.
Thu Jul 30Final poster + write-up due — print-ready PDF to program coordinator.
Thu Jul 31REU Closing Ceremony & Poster Presentation

Assignment Overview

WeekAssignmentDueFormat
1First pipeline run + 1-page reflectionFri Jun 27CSV + Google Doc
2Python exercises (1–8) + error analysis + project selectionFri Jul 3Notebook + Google Doc + Slack message
3Project proposal (oral Wed) + 1-page project summary (written Fri)Wed Jul 9 · Fri Jul 11Google Doc
4Mid-point pitch (5 min) + experiment log with incremental resultsWed Jul 16 · Fri Jul 18Google Doc + results CSV
5Poster draft + 2-page write-up draft + final results CSVFri Jul 25PDF/Slides + Google Doc
6Poster inspection Mon Jul 28 · Final poster + write-up Thu Jul 30Mon Jul 28 · Thu Jul 30Print-ready PDF + Google Doc

Assignment Details

Week 1 — First Pipeline Run + Reflection

Pipeline run: Run batch_runner.py on 5 rows from the goldcoin dataset. Save to results/[yourname]_week1.csv and commit to GitHub.

Reflection (1 page): Answer these 3 questions with roughly 1 paragraph each:

  • What surprised you most about ComplianceGPT?
  • Did any of your 5 results surprise you? Why?
  • What question about AI and compliance are you most curious about?

Graded on engagement, not correctness.

Week 2 — Python Exercises + Error Analysis + Project Selection

Exercises 1–8 in notebooks/week2_nlp.ipynb: token counting, batch accuracy, precision/recall, results filtering, plotting a bar chart.

Error analysis (1 page): Choose 2 wrong predictions from your batch. For each: summarize the scenario, identify what field was wrong, propose a fix.

Project Selection — Due Friday Jul 3

By the end of Week 2 you must choose your project (P1–P6 from the Student Projects page). Send Priscilla a Slack message with:

  1. Which project you're picking (e.g. "P3 — Adversarial Robustness")
  2. Your draft research question in one sentence
  3. One sentence on why you picked it

This is a commitment, not a final contract. If the project turns out to be infeasible we'll adjust in Week 3. But you need a direction before proposal week.

Week 3 — Project Proposal + 1-Page Project Summary

Oral presentation (5 min) — Wednesday Jul 9: Informal pitch of your selected project. Cover four things: (1) the problem, (2) your specific research question, (3) your planned method, (4) how you'll know if it worked.

1-page project summary — due Friday Jul 11: The written version of your proposal. Must include all of the following:

  • Research question (1 sentence — specific and answerable)
  • Background (2–3 sentences — why does this matter, what's the gap)
  • Method (bullet list — what experiments you will run, what data, what metric)
  • Expected result (your hypothesis — what do you predict and why)
  • Incremental milestones:
    • Week 4 milestone: what will you have completed by Jul 18?
    • Week 5 milestone: what will you have completed by Jul 25?
    • Week 6: poster ready for inspection

Graded on specificity. "I will run experiments" is not a milestone. "I will run the baseline prompt on 50 adversarial scenarios and compute ASR" is.

Week 4 — Mid-Point Pitch + Experiment Log with Incremental Results

Mid-point pitch (5 min) — Wednesday Jul 16: Show actual results, even if preliminary. Four-part structure:

  1. Done: what experiments have you run?
  2. Found: what are the numbers? (show a table or chart)
  3. Blocked: what is not working or unclear?
  4. Plan: exactly what will you do in Weeks 5–6 to finish?

Experiment log — due Friday Jul 18: A running log of every experiment you ran in Weeks 3–4. For each experiment record:

  • Date, configuration (model, prompt version, dataset slice)
  • Numeric result (accuracy, ASR, F1, or whatever your metric is)
  • 1-sentence observation: what did this tell you?
  • 1-sentence next step: what did you change or decide as a result?

Minimum 5 logged experiments. Include at least 1 table or chart summarizing results so far.

The experiment log documents your thinking, not just your successes. A failed experiment that taught you something is more valuable than a lucky result you can't explain.

Week 5 — Poster Draft + 2-Page Write-Up Draft

Poster draft — due Friday Jul 25: All sections must be present and filled in (not placeholder text). Your main result must be visible as a table or chart. Design does not need to be final — content must be complete enough for substantive feedback.

2-page write-up draft — due Friday Jul 25: All 5 sections present (introduction, background, method, results, discussion/conclusion). References required. Feedback returned by Monday Jul 28 morning.

Final results CSV — due Friday Jul 25: Complete experimental results committed to results/[yourname]_final.csv. No more experiments after this — Week 6 is writing and presentation only.

Week 6 — Poster Inspection + Final Deliverables

Poster Inspection — Monday Jul 28

Before your poster goes to print, Priscilla will review it with you 1:1. We will check: readability at 6 feet, that the main result is prominent, that the title and conclusion match, and that affiliation and acknowledgment lines are correct. Plan on 15–20 minutes. Schedule your slot by Friday Jul 25.

After inspection: incorporate feedback and resubmit by Tuesday Jul 29 at noon. The coordinator needs time to print before Thursday.

Final poster (print-ready PDF) — due Thursday Jul 30: 48×36 inches. Font minimums: 24pt body, 36pt headers, 56pt title. Submit to the REU program coordinator.

Final write-up — due Thursday Jul 30: Polished 2-page research summary incorporating Week 5 feedback. Goes in the REU portfolio and may become the basis for a publication.

Poster presentation — Thursday Jul 31: REU Closing Ceremony. Stand with your printed poster and explain your work to faculty, other students, and guests. You should be able to give a 2-minute summary without notes.

Final Poster

Poster Requirements

Size48 × 36 inches (landscape)
Font (body)Minimum 24pt
Font (headers)Minimum 36pt
Font (title)Minimum 56pt
SectionsTitle, Introduction, Background, Method, Results, Conclusion, References
FiguresAt least 1 table or chart (your main result)
AffiliationStony Brook University, ComplianceGPT Lab, AI Innovation & Diffusion REU 2026
DueJuly 30 (print-ready PDF to program coordinator)

Poster Tools

  • PowerPoint / Google Slides — set slide size to 48×36 inches. Most accessible option.
  • Canva — free, clean templates, easy to use. Export as PDF at high resolution.
  • LaTeX beamerposter — for students who know LaTeX. Produces the cleanest output.

Poster Design Tips

  • Use the 6-foot test: stand 6 feet back. Can you read the title? The main result? If not, increase font size.
  • Your main result should be the largest visual element — a table or chart in the center column.
  • Every figure must have a caption with a 1-sentence takeaway starting with the finding.
  • Maximum 5 sentences per section panel. Cut everything else — bullets are fine.
  • Colors: SBU crimson (#990000) and navy (#003366) if you want brand consistency.

Publication Opportunities

Who gets invited to co-author?

Students whose projects produce a novel finding — not just a replication of existing results. A finding is novel if: (1) the specific comparison hasn't been published, (2) the dataset (adversarial scenarios, annotation rubric) is new, or (3) the result is surprising and well-explained. You don't need to solve a major open problem — you need to answer your research question clearly and credibly.

Target Venues

ACL / EMNLP / NAACL Student Research Workshop

The top NLP conferences each have student workshops with a lower bar than the main track. Good fit for 4-page papers from REU projects. Peer reviewed, published proceedings, presentation opportunity.

AI + Law Workshops

Workshops co-located with ICAIL, JURIX, or ACL that focus specifically on AI for legal tasks. Our work fits here directly. Often more accessible for shorter papers.

AAAI Undergraduate/Student Track

AAAI has an undergraduate consortium. If your result is strong and you're an undergraduate, this is a good venue. National conference with high visibility.

arXiv Preprint

Even if not submitted to a conference, a polished 4-page arXiv paper is a real artifact. It's citable, shareable, and evidence of research output for graduate school applications.

The co-authorship process

If your project qualifies:

  1. I tell you before Week 6 ends — "this is publishable, let's write it up."
  2. I take your 2-page write-up and expand it to a 4–8 page submission format.
  3. You review drafts and revise your sections (method, your specific results).
  4. I handle submission logistics, formatting, and correspondence.
  5. Your authorship: first author on the portion you led; co-author on a paper with multiple contributors.

Timeline: initial paper draft ~2 months after the program ends. Expect 2–3 revision rounds over 2–4 weeks total.
